name: Biondi, Francesco N.
recent_1: Sahoo, P., & Biondi, F. N. (2026). On the effect of using an augmented reality laser projection operator guidance system on cognitive workload and assembly task performance. Applied Ergonomics, 130, 104662. DOI: 10.1016/j.apergo.2025.104662
recent_1_synopsis: This study tested whether an augmented-reality laser projection system that overlays assembly instructions directly onto the work surface reduces operator cognitive workload and improves performance relative to conventional guidance. Laser projection lowered cognitive workload and yielded faster task completion, though the advantage disappeared under higher cognitive task demands. The work extends Biondi's workload-assessment program from driving into industrial Industry 5.0 manufacturing contexts.
recent_2: Biondi, F. N., Bashir, S., Yujin, K., & Horrobin, A. (2025). Driver distraction in school zones: A roadside observational study in Canada. Transportation Research Part F: Traffic Psychology and Behaviour, 115, 103326. DOI: 10.1016/j.trf.2025.07.036
recent_2_synopsis: Through roadside observation of nearly 5,000 vehicles at seven Windsor, Ontario school zones during morning drop-off and afternoon pick-up, the authors quantified the prevalence of distracted and impatient driving in a high-risk pedestrian environment. Roughly 20% of drivers showed distracting behaviours in fall (about 16% in winter), and drivers of larger vehicles such as pickups and SUVs were more likely to engage in risky or illegal behaviour. The study provides naturalistic field evidence to inform school-zone safety policy and enforcement.
recent_3: Vasta, N., & Biondi, F. N. (2025). Effect of Partially Automated Driving on Mental Workload, Visual Behavior and Engagement in Nondriving-Related Tasks: A Meta-Analysis. Human Factors, 67(9), 962-991. DOI: 10.1177/00187208251323132
recent_3_synopsis: This meta-analysis synthesizes a body of mixed findings on whether partial driving automation raises or lowers driver mental workload, and how it reshapes visual scanning and engagement in non-driving-related tasks. The authors reconcile conflicting results across studies and clarify the conditions under which partial automation imposes suboptimal versus manual-like workload. It offers a consolidated evidence base for designing and evaluating driver monitoring and automation systems.
seminal_1: Strayer, D. L., Turrill, J., Cooper, J. M., Coleman, J. R., Medeiros-Ward, N., & Biondi, F. (2015). Assessing Cognitive Distraction in the Automobile. Human Factors, 57(8), 1300-1324. DOI: 10.1177/0018720815575149
seminal_1_synopsis: Across three studies in which participants performed eight common in-vehicle tasks, this paper established a systematic framework and a cognitive distraction scale by integrating primary-task, secondary-task, subjective, and physiological measures (including the Detection Response Task). It is Biondi's most influential work, cited approximately 203 times (Crossref, June 2026; Google Scholar reports a higher count), and it became a foundational reference for quantifying the mental workload of voice-based and infotainment interactions, demonstrating that hands-free/eyes-on-road interactions can still be cognitively demanding.
seminal_2: Biondi, F. N., Cacanindin, A., Douglas, C., & Cort, J. (2020). Overloaded and at Work: Investigating the Effect of Cognitive Workload on Assembly Task Performance. Human Factors, 62(5), 813-825. DOI: 10.1177/0018720820929928
seminal_2_synopsis: This experiment showed that elevated cognitive workload degrades performance on a manual assembly task, transferring driving-derived workload-assessment methods (the Detection Response Task) into occupational and manufacturing settings. With roughly 87 citations (per the pre-verified seed list), it is a foundational piece in extending cognitive-workload measurement beyond driving into industrial human factors and ergonomics, anchoring Biondi's later Industry 5.0 work.
expertise_overview: Biondi studies driver cognitive workload, distraction, and attention allocation, with a central focus on the costs of secondary tasks such as voice-based and infotainment interactions while driving. A core methodological contribution is his use and refinement of the ISO Detection Response Task and other stimulus-detection and physiological measures to quantify mental workload in real time. He examines the human factors of partial and full driving automation, including vigilance decrement, situation awareness, glance allocation, and engagement in non-driving-related tasks, and he develops and evaluates driver monitoring systems. More recently he has extended workload-assessment methods into occupational and Industry 5.0 contexts such as assembly tasks, augmented-reality operator guidance, and exoskeleton-assisted work. His methods span driving simulators, on-road and naturalistic field studies, roadside observation, eye tracking, and meta-analysis, applied across automotive safety, manufacturing, and human-automation interaction.
notes: Identity confidence: high. Confirmed as Francesco N. Biondi, Associate Professor (Department of Kinesiology) and Director of the Human Systems Lab, University of Windsor; previously Research Scientist at the University of Utah with David L. Strayer, and Human Factors Engineer at Jaguar Land Rover; PhD in psychological science, University of Padova (2015). Sources confirming identity (accessed June 2026): University of Windsor / WE-SPARK profile, ResearchGate (Francesco-Biondi), and Google Scholar profile (user=y1sLUXIAAAAJ; ~2,813 total citations, h-index ~25). All six DOIs cited (recent_1, recent_2, recent_3, seminal_1, seminal_2) were verified via the Crossref REST API on 2026-06-17 with matching titles, venues, and dates. Citation counts: seminal_1 = 203 (Crossref is-referenced-by-count, 2026-06-17; Google Scholar reports a higher number); recent_3 = 7 (Crossref); seminal_2 = ~87 (from the pre-verified seed list rather than re-counted). No unverifiable citations were used and no DOIs were invented. Note: recent_2 author-name 'Yujin, K.' reflects the order returned by Crossref author metadata and may not be the fully canonical given/family split.
